1	# Analogy: monoidal structure / associator on `SheafOfModules R` via sheafification
2	
3	## Mode
4	cross-domain-inspiration
5	
6	## Slug
7	snap-assoc
8	
9	## Iteration
10	048
11	
12	## Structural problem (abstracted)
13	`C` monoidal, `W ⊆ Mor C` a class of weak equivalences, `L : C ⥤ D` the
14	reflective localization at `W` (left adjoint to a fully faithful inclusion).
15	We want a monoidal structure on `D` (in particular an associator) descended
16	from `C`. This is the strong-monoidality comparison `L P ⊗ L Q ≅ L(P ⊗ Q)`,
17	equivalently `IsIso (L.map (η_P ▷ Q))` for the localization unit `η`.
18	Here `C = PresheafOfModules R₀`, `D = SheafOfModules R`, `L = sheafification`.
19	
20	## Failed approaches (from directive)
21	- Stalkwise/locally-bijective ⟹ IsIso applied to `η_P ⊗ 𝟙_Q`: tensor only
22	  right-exact, so `η_P ⊗ 𝟙` is not locally injective. (Correct diagnosis — see
23	  why this is the wrong object to test below.)
24	- Direct `MonoidalCategory (SheafOfModules R)` instance: absent in pinned Mathlib.
25	- `Localization.Monoidal` exists but not instantiated for module sheafification.
26	
27	## Key Mathlib facts established this iter
28	- **`PresheafOfModules.sheafification α` IS a localization functor**:
29	  `(sheafification α).IsLocalization (J.W.inverseImage (toPresheaf R₀))`
30	  — `Mathlib/Algebra/Category/ModuleCat/Sheaf/Localization.lean:48`.
31	  So the `L.IsLocalization W` prerequisite of `LocalizedMonoidal` is already met,
32	  with `W = J.W.inverseImage (toPresheaf R₀)`.
33	- `PresheafOfModules (R ⋙ forget₂)` is monoidal (needs `R : Cᵒᵖ ⥤ CommRingCat`):
34	  `Mathlib/Algebra/Category/ModuleCat/Presheaf/Monoidal.lean` (tensor = objectwise
35	  `M₁.obj X ⊗_{R(X)} M₂.obj X`).
36	- **Absent in Mathlib** (the real gaps): `MonoidalClosed (PresheafOfModules R)`;
37	  `(J.W.inverseImage (toPresheaf R₀)).IsMonoidal`; any `MonoidalCategory (SheafOfModules R)`
38	  or `LocalizedMonoidal` instance for modules; stalk infrastructure for module sheaves.
39	
40	## Analogues found (ranked by porting cost, lowest first)
41	
42	### Analogue 1: `CategoryTheory.Sites.Monoidal` + `CategoryTheory.Localization.Monoidal.Basic`
43	- **Domain**: category theory / sheaf theory (the direct template — Joël Riou).
44	- **Same problem there**: `MonoidalCategory (Sheaf J A)` for a *fixed* monoidal
45	  target `A`, built as `LocalizedMonoidal (L := presheafToSheaf J A) (W := J.W) (Iso.refl _)`
46	  — `Sites/Monoidal.lean:165` (`Sheaf.monoidalCategory`). The `LocalizedMonoidal`
47	  machinery (`Localization/Monoidal/Basic.lean:86`) needs only: `MonoidalCategory C`,
48	  `L.IsLocalization W`, `W.IsMonoidal`, a unit iso `ε`. The associator/unitors/braiding
49	  all come from the localization-of-trifunctors machinery for free.
50	- **Technique**: the *sole* obligation `J.W.IsMonoidal` (whiskering preserves weak
51	  equivalences) is discharged in `GrothendieckTopology.W.monoidal` (`Sites/Monoidal.lean:149`)
52	  via `W.whiskerLeft` (`:132`): `g ∈ W ⟺ ∀ sheaf H, Hom(-,H)` bijective; to show
53	  `F ◁ g ∈ W`, transport across the **tensor–hom adjunction** `Hom(F⊗Gᵢ,H) ≅ Hom(Gᵢ,[F,H])`
54	  using that **the internal hom `[F,H]` is itself a sheaf** (`isSheaf_functorEnrichedHom`,
55	  `:102`). `whiskerRight` is derived from `whiskerLeft` by the braiding (`:144`).
56	  NOT a stalk argument — no exactness ever invoked.
57	- **Mapping to project**: instantiate `LocalizedMonoidal` with `L = PresheafOfModules.sheafification α`,
58	  `W = J.W.inverseImage (toPresheaf R₀)` (already `IsLocalization`, Localization.lean:48),
59	  `ε : sheafification(𝟙) ≅ unitModule`. Then `MonoidalCategory (SheafOfModules R)` and the
60	  associator drop out. Sole obligation: `(J.W.inverseImage (toPresheaf R₀)).IsMonoidal`.
61	- **Why it is NOT free**: the generic "inverse image under a *monoidal* functor of a
62	  monoidal `W` is monoidal" instance (`Localization/Monoidal/Basic.lean:71`) does NOT
63	  apply, because `toPresheaf : PresheafOfModules R₀ ⥤ (Cᵒᵖ ⥤ AddCommGrp)` is NOT strong
64	  monoidal (module tensor `⊗_{R₀}` ≠ underlying abelian tensor `⊗_ℤ`). So `W.IsMonoidal`
65	  cannot be reduced to the (already proven) abelian `J.W.IsMonoidal`. It must be proven at
66	  the module level — by the closed lever (Analogue 2) or stalks (which need absent infra).
67	- **Porting cost**: medium-high. The `LocalizedMonoidal` wiring is short; the cost is
68	  entirely in `W.IsMonoidal`, which routes through Analogue 2.
69	- **Verdict**: ANALOGUE_FOUND.
70	
71	### Analogue 2: `CategoryTheory.Monoidal.Reflective.isIso_tfae` (Day's reflection theorem)
72	- **Domain**: category theory (reflective subcategories / closed monoidal).
73	- **Same problem there**: `D` symmetric monoidal closed, `C ↪ D` reflective with
74	  reflector `L`. `Monoidal/Braided/Reflection.lean:92` proves TFAE:
75	  (1) `∀ c d, IsIso (η.app ((ihom d).obj (R.obj c)))`,
76	  (2) `∀ c d, IsIso ((pre (η.app d)).app (R.obj c))`,
77	  (3) `∀ d d', IsIso (L.map (η.app d ▷ d'))`,
78	  (4) `∀ d d', IsIso (L.map (η.app d ⊗ₘ η.app d'))`.
79	  Condition (4) is EXACTLY the strong-monoidal comparison; (3) is exactly the
80	  directive's `L.map (η_P ▷ Q)`. And `Reflection.lean:242` gives `monoidalClosed C`
81	  (the reflective subcategory is itself closed monoidal).
82	- **Technique**: condition (1) — **"the internal hom into a local (sheaf) object is
83	  itself local (a sheaf)"** — is the cheap-to-verify end of the TFAE and implies (3)/(4).
84	  This is the abstract form of `isSheaf_functorEnrichedHom`. The proof builds an explicit
85	  retraction of the unit (`adjRetraction`, `:53`) from the tensor-hom data.
86	- **Mapping to project**: `D = PresheafOfModules R` (must be `SymmetricCategory` —
87	  true over a sheaf of *commutative* rings — and `MonoidalClosed` — ABSENT), `C =
88	  SheafOfModules R`, reflective adjunction = `sheafificationAdjunction` (have it). Verify
89	  condition (1): internal hom of presheaves-of-modules `[F,Q]` is a sheaf when `Q` is.
90	  Then (3)/(4) give the strong-monoidal comparison, discharging `W.IsMonoidal` of Analogue 1,
91	  AND `monoidalClosed (SheafOfModules R)` for free.
92	- **Porting cost**: high. Prerequisite = build `MonoidalClosed (PresheafOfModules R)`
93	  (internal hom of module presheaves; Mathlib has `Monoidal.Closed.FunctorCategory.Basic`
94	  and `ModuleCat.Monoidal.Closed` to assemble from, but PresheafOfModules carries
95	  restriction-of-scalars in its transition maps, so it is not a plain functor category) +
96	  prove `[F,Q]` preserves the sheaf condition.
97	- **Verdict**: ANALOGUE_FOUND (the robust, site-general route; subsumes Analogue 1's gap).
98	
99	### Analogue 3: doctrinal adjunction + base-change strong monoidality
100	- **Citations**: `CategoryTheory.Adjunction.rightAdjointLaxMonoidal` /
101	  `leftAdjointOplaxMonoidal`; `ModuleCat` `(extendScalars f).Monoidal`
102	  (`Algebra/Category/ModuleCat/Monoidal/Adjunction.lean:42`) and the induced
103	  `(restrictScalars f).LaxMonoidal` (`:102`).
104	- **Domain**: commutative algebra (the algebraic shadow of sheafification-tensor).
105	- **Same problem there**: extension of scalars `S ⊗_R - : ModuleCat R ⥤ ModuleCat S`
106	  (a base-change "localization-flavored" left adjoint) is STRONG monoidal — `μ` is the
107	  canonical projection-formula iso `S⊗_R(M⊗_R N) ≅ (S⊗M)⊗_S(S⊗N)`, packaged via
108	  `Functor.CoreMonoidal.toMonoidal`. Its right adjoint `restrictScalars` is then
109	  automatically LAX monoidal by `Adjunction.rightAdjointLaxMonoidal`.
110	- **Technique**: (a) `CoreMonoidal` — build `μ`/`ε` from the one canonical comparison iso
111	  and let the machinery supply coherence; (b) doctrinal adjunction — a right adjoint of a
112	  (op/strong) monoidal functor is lax monoidal for free.
113	- **Mapping to project**: two uses. (i) The lax-monoidal **global sections Γ** of the
114	  section graded ring (`Γ(F)⊗Γ(G)→Γ(F⊗G)`) is exactly `rightAdjointLaxMonoidal`/structure
115	  map — get it for free once `SheafOfModules` is monoidal and `Γ` is a right adjoint.
116	  (ii) `CoreMonoidal` is the build pattern for the bespoke line-bundle comparison (Analogue 4):
117	  supply the single iso `μ_{m,m'}`, derive coherence.
118	- **Porting cost**: low-medium (these are immediate once a monoidal structure exists;
119	  primarily a pattern to reuse, not the crux).
120	- **Verdict**: PARTIAL_ANALOGUE (technique/pattern, not the crux).
121	
122	### Analogue 4: bespoke tensor-power comparison on line bundles (NO full MonoidalCategory)
123	- **Domain**: assembled technique (locally-free modules + `mapIso` + `CoreMonoidal` pattern).
124	- **Same problem there**: the project's ACTUAL `snap-assoc` need is associativity of the
125	  section graded ring `⊕_m Γ(X, L^{⊗m})`, which needs `tensorPowAdd : L^{⊗m} ⊗ L^{⊗m'} ≅
126	  L^{⊗(m+m')}` and its pentagon/associativity — i.e. the associator only on tensor POWERS
127	  of an invertible/line bundle `L`, NOT on all of `SheafOfModules R`.
128	- **Technique**: for *locally free* factors the strong-monoidal comparison
129	  `L(P)⊗L(Q) ≅ L(P⊗Q)` is an iso for trivial reasons — locally `O⊗O = O`, so the presheaf
130	  tensor is already locally a free module and its sheafification unit is locally the
131	  identity; right-exactness is never invoked (this is exactly why failed-approach #1's
132	  obstruction evaporates: the bad object `η_P⊗𝟙` is never the thing tested — on locally
133	  free pieces the comparison is a genuine local iso). Build `μ_{m,m'}` by induction on
134	  tensor powers from the right-unitor + one comparison iso; prove associativity as a `μ`
135	  cocycle (pentagon) directly.
136	- **Mapping to project**: extend `SectionGradedRing.lean` (already has `tensorObj`,
137	  `tensorPow`, `unitModule`, unitor, braiding via `mapIso`) with `μ_{m,m'}` and a pentagon
138	  lemma, restricted to invertible `L`. Avoids `MonoidalCategory (SheafOfModules R)` and
139	  `MonoidalClosed` entirely.
140	- **Porting cost**: lowest. Self-contained in the project file; does not generalize.
141	- **Verdict**: ANALOGUE_FOUND (pragmatic unblock for snap-assoc specifically).
142	
143	## Top suggestion
144	Two-track. **To unblock `snap-assoc` cheaply now**: Analogue 4 — build the tensor-power
145	comparison family `μ_{m,m'} : L^{⊗m} ⊗ L^{⊗m'} ≅ L^{⊗(m+m')}` by induction in
146	`AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/SectionGradedRing.lean`, using locally-free-ness so the
147	strong-monoidal comparison is a local iso (right-exactness never invoked), and prove the
148	pentagon as a cocycle. **For the principled full structure**: Analogue 1 — instantiate
149	`LocalizedMonoidal (L := PresheafOfModules.sheafification α) (W := J.W.inverseImage
150	(toPresheaf R₀)) ε` (read `Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Sites/Monoidal.lean` and
151	`Mathlib/Algebra/Category/ModuleCat/Sheaf/Localization.lean:48`); its sole obligation
152	`(J.W.inverseImage (toPresheaf R₀)).IsMonoidal` is discharged via Analogue 2 (Day's
153	`isIso_tfae` condition (1): internal hom of module-presheaves into a sheaf is a sheaf),
154	which additionally yields `MonoidalClosed (SheafOfModules R)`. The single genuinely missing
155	Mathlib brick for the principled route is `MonoidalClosed (PresheafOfModules R)`
156	(+ internal-hom-preserves-sheaf) — that is the `NEEDS_MATHLIB_GAP_FILL` core.
157	
158	## Discarded
159	- Generic monoidal-functor inverse-image instance (`Localization/Monoidal/Basic.lean:71`):
160	  inapplicable — `toPresheaf` is not strong monoidal (`⊗_{R₀} ≠ ⊗_ℤ`).
161	- Objectwise/locally-bijective `W.IsMonoidal`: matches failed-approach #1 — tensor only
162	  right-exact, local injectivity of `X◁g` fails.
163	- Pure stalk route (`isIso_iff_stalkFunctor_map_iso`, `Topology/Sheaves/Stalks.lean:652`):
164	  would work on a space (`g∈W ⟺ stalkwise iso`; `(F◁g)_x = id⊗g_x` iso since `F_x⊗-`
165	  preserves the iso `g_x`, no exactness), but stalk-of-tensor and stalk infra for sheaves
166	  of modules are ABSENT in Mathlib — building them ≳ the closed-lever route.

64	# Blueprint Writer
65	
66	You write or revise **one blueprint chapter** under plan-agent direction. You receive a precise directive naming the target chapter, the strategy context the chapter must reflect, the definitions/theorems that must be present, and the scope of changes you may make.
67	
68	## Your Job
69	
70	The plan agent has decided that a specific blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex`) needs to change — to reflect a strategic decision, fill a missing definition or theorem the project needs, or align the informal prose with the current Lean structure. Your directive tells you which chapter, what must be there, and what counts as out-of-scope.
71	
72	You only edit the **one chapter named in your directive**. Your declared `--write-domain` should reflect that (`blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex`). You do NOT edit other chapters, `.lean` files, or `content.tex`. Cross-chapter inconsistencies you spot go in the report's "Notes for Plan Agent" section — you flag them, you don't fix them.
73	
74	## Directive Format
75	
76	```markdown
77	# Blueprint Writer Directive
78	
79	## Slug
80	<slug>
81	
82	## Target chapter
83	blueprint/src/chapters/<chapter-slug>.tex
84	
85	## Strategy context
86	<the relevant slice of STRATEGY.md the plan agent extracted for you — typically a paragraph or two describing what this chapter must support in the overall arc. You do NOT read STRATEGY.md yourself; the plan agent gives you only what is relevant.>
87	
88	## Required content
89	- Definition <name>: <informal description of what it must define, with the mathematical content the prover needs in order to formalize>
90	- Theorem <name>: <statement + intent of the proof sketch the prover should formalize>
91	- Proof sketch for <theorem>: <how detailed; what cross-references; whether to expand a step into sub-lemmas>
92	- ...
93	
94	## Out of scope
95	<things that look related but the plan agent does NOT want you to touch this round>
96	
97	## References
98	- <path/to/reference.md or arxiv ID>: <which sections are relevant>
99	- ...
100	
101	## Expected outcome
102	<what the chapter should look like after, in one paragraph>
103	```
104	
105	If a section is omitted from your directive, the plan agent decided you don't need it. Don't speculate beyond what was given.
106	
107	## Chapter format
108	
109	Each declaration block in a chapter looks like:
110	
111	```latex
112	\begin{theorem}[name_for_humans]
113	  \label{thm:some_label}
114	  \lean{namespace.theorem_name}
115	  \uses{def:related_definition, lem:supporting_lemma}
116	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], III.5.1, p. 174  (read from references/hartshorne-III-5.md)
117	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "A morphism $f: X \to Y$ of schemes locally of finite
118	  % type is said to be smooth at $x \in X$ if there exist an open affine
119	  % neighborhood $V = \Spec B$ of $f(x)$ and an open affine neighborhood
120	  % $U = \Spec A$ of $x$ with $f(U) \subset V$ such that ..."
121	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, III.5.1.}
122	  Informal statement of the theorem, in the project's notation.
123	\end{theorem}
124	
125	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Proof. We may assume $Y = \Spec B$ and
126	% $X = \Spec A$ are affine. Then $f$ corresponds to a ring homomorphism
127	% $\varphi: B \to A$, and $f$ is smooth at $x$ if and only if ..."
128	\begin{proof}
129	  \uses{thm:another_result}
130	  Step-by-step informal proof, in the project's notation. Reference blueprint labels
131	  with \uses{...} so the dependency graph stays accurate.
132	\end{proof}
133	```
134	
135	Use `\definition`, `\lemma`, `\theorem`, `\proposition`, `\corollary` as appropriate. `\lean{...}` names the Lean declaration this block corresponds to. `\uses{...}` records cross-references; keep it accurate so `leanblueprint`'s dependency graph remains usable.
136	
137	### Citation discipline (the hard rule)
138	
139	**Every declaration block that derives from external reference material requires three citation elements:**
140	
141	1. **`% SOURCE:` LaTeX comment** — pointer + local file. Format: `% SOURCE: <citation pointer> (read from references/<file>.md)`. The pointer is the source identifier + section/theorem/definition number + page when available (e.g. `[Hartshorne], III.5.1, p. 174` or `[Stacks Project], Tag 01V4`). The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical names the local file you opened to produce the verbatim quote.
142	2. **`% SOURCE QUOTE:` LaTeX comment** — the **verbatim text** of the cited statement, copied character-by-character from the local reference file you just named in the `% SOURCE:` line. Verbatim means:
143	   - **Original language.** French for Bourbaki / EGA, German for Grothendieck's early notes, English for Hartshorne / Vakil / Stacks, etc. Do NOT translate.
144	   - **Original notation preserved.** If the source writes $\mathcal{O}_X^*$ and the project uses $\mathcal{O}_X^\times$, the verbatim quote keeps $\mathcal{O}_X^*$. The project-notation restatement happens in the prose body that follows, NOT in the quote.
145	   - **Every word, every symbol.** No paraphrase, no "for brevity" abbreviation. The quote is the anti-hallucination signal — a writer who actually opened the source can paste; a writer reconstructing from memory cannot reproduce the source's exact words.
146	3. **Visible `\textit{Source: <pointer>.}` line** — first line of the prose body. Renders in the PDF so a mathematician reading the typeset blueprint sees the citation at a glance.
147	
148	**For proof blocks**: include a `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` LaTeX comment **immediately before** the `\begin{proof}` environment (NOT inside it). Same verbatim rules — original language, original notation, every word. The informal proof body inside `\begin{proof}...\end{proof}` is the project's restated version in project notation, what the prover formalizes.
149	
150	When a source proof is too long for one verbatim block (multi-page construction): split the theorem into sub-lemmas in the directive's logical structure, and give each sub-statement its own `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`. One opaque mega-quote defeats verifiability. If even sub-splitting is impractical, report it in "Notes for Plan Agent" — do not silently drop the verbatim quote.
151	
152	**For Archon-original / project-bespoke results** (the directive does not name an external source for this block): the source lines are omitted — the block stands on the proof sketch alone.

154	**The hard rule, explicit:**
155	
156	> **You may NEVER write `% SOURCE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`, or `\textit{Source: ...}` from training memory.** Every such line must be backed by a local file under `references/` that you have **opened and read in this session**, and the verbatim quote must be a character-by-character copy of text from that file.

158	The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical in `% SOURCE:` is your discipline check. If, when reviewing your own draft, you find a block where you cannot point to the specific local file you read to produce the quote — you fabricated it. Delete the quote. Either dispatch a `reference-retriever` (see below) to obtain the missing source, or report the block as INCOMPLETE under "Notes for Plan Agent".

161

205	## Mathlib dependency anchors (`\mathlibok`)
206	
207	When your chapter's proofs rely on a definition or lemma that **Mathlib already provides** (and the project will not re-prove), it is good practice — not just a bare `\uses{}` to nowhere — to make that dependency a first-class blueprint block: a **Mathlib dependency anchor**. Doing so:
208	
209	- makes the reliance explicit and readable (a mathematician sees exactly which Mathlib result the route stands on);
210	- gives `leandag` a real node, so the `\uses{}` resolves (no broken/unknown ref) and the node's effort is **done** rather than ∞;
211	- keeps it clear how much the route leans on Mathlib vs. on original work.
212	
213	Write the anchor like any statement block, with three differences:
214	
215	```latex
216	\begin{lemma}[Hensel's lemma]
217	  \label{lem:hensel_mathlib}
218	  \lean{Mathlib.RingTheory.Henselian.foo}   % the REAL Mathlib declaration
219	  \mathlibok
220	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
221	  Statement of the result in the project's notation.
222	\end{lemma}
223	```
224	
225	- **`\lean{}` names the actual Mathlib declaration** (with its real namespace), so `leandag` matches the node to Mathlib.
226	- **`\mathlibok`** marks it as supplied by Mathlib — the DAG treats it as done; no `\begin{proof}` is needed.
227	- No `% SOURCE:`/`% SOURCE QUOTE:` block — Mathlib is the source; the `\lean{}` target *is* the citation.
228	
229	**The anti-hallucination rule (mirrors citation discipline):** mark `\mathlibok` ONLY when the statement genuinely exists in Mathlib and your stated form is faithful to it. A wrong or invented `\mathlibok` is worse than an ∞ hole — the loop will treat a non-existent result as available and skip proving a real gap. So:
230	
231	- Prefer anchors the **directive names** (the plan agent/reviewer identified them as Mathlib results).
232	- If you are not confident the exact statement is in Mathlib, do **not** mark `\mathlibok` — write the block without it (or leave the `\uses{}` for the reviewer to resolve) and flag it under "Notes for Plan Agent" so the reviewer can verify against Lean.
233	- Never put `\mathlibok` on one of the project's **own** to-be-proved declarations. That marker is for Mathlib-supplied results only; `\leanok` (project proofs) is never yours to add.

1	\chapter{Section graded ring infrastructure: tensor powers and graded sections}
2	\label{chap:Picard_SectionGradedRing}
3	
4	% This chapter blueprints the Mathlib-absent infrastructure that the section
5	% graded ring R(X_s,L_s)=⊕_m Γ(X_s,L_s^{⊗m}) and section graded module
6	% M(X_s,F_s,L_s)=⊕_m Γ(X_s,F_s⊗L_s^{⊗m}) of Picard_QuotScheme.tex
7	% (def:sectionGradedRing / def:sectionGradedModule) depend on. Those two
8	% definitions are NOT restated here; this chapter supplies the three separable
9	% infrastructure layers they \uses{}. The target Lean file is
10	% AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/SectionGradedRing.lean (1:1 slug, to be scaffolded).
11	
12	\providecommand{\PshMod}{\mathrm{PresheafOfModules}}
13	\providecommand{\ShMod}{\mathrm{SheafOfModules}}
14	\providecommand{\Modules}{\mathrm{Modules}}
15	
16	The Hilbert-polynomial route of \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme} represents the
17	degree-\(m\) graded component of a fibre by the global sections
18	\(\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) of a twist of a coherent
19	sheaf by a tensor power of a line bundle, and it organises these components into
20	a graded ring \(R(X_s, L_s) = \bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\)
21	(\cref{def:sectionGradedRing}) and a graded module
22	\(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s) = \bigoplus_{m \ge 0}
23	\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\)
24	(\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}). Three pieces of that picture have no direct
25	Mathlib counterpart at the pinned commit and must be built here, each in its own
26	section:
27	
28	\begin{enumerate}
29	  \item \textbf{Tensor powers of a sheaf of modules}
30	    (\cref{sec:sgr_tensor_powers}). Mathlib equips the category
31	    \(\PshMod\) of \emph{presheaves} of modules over a presheaf of commutative
32	    rings with a symmetric monoidal structure
33	    (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}) and provides the
34	    sheafification functor \(\PshMod \to \ShMod\)
35	    (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}); but the category
36	    \(\ShMod\) of \emph{sheaves} of modules carries \emph{no} monoidal
37	    structure. The tensor product of sheaves of modules
38	    \(\mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}\) must therefore be built
39	    as the sheafification of the pointwise tensor presheaf, and the iterated
40	    tensor power \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m}\) recursively from it.
41	  \item \textbf{Lax-monoidal global sections} (\cref{sec:sgr_lax_sections}).
42	    The global-sections functor \(\Gamma\) is only \emph{lax} monoidal: from the
43	    pointwise-strict monoidality of the presheaf tensor product and the
44	    sheafification unit one obtains a natural multiplication
45	    \(\Gamma(\mathcal{F}) \otimes_{\Gamma(\mathcal{O}_X)} \Gamma(\mathcal{G})
46	    \to \Gamma(\mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})\), together with
47	    the associativity and unit coherence those products must satisfy.
48	  \item \textbf{Graded-ring / graded-module assembly}
49	    (\cref{sec:sgr_graded_assembly}). The section multiplications and the
50	    tensor-power comparison isomorphisms assemble, through Mathlib's
51	    external-direct-sum graded API (\cref{lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib},
52	    \cref{lem:directSum_gmodule_mathlib}), into the graded \emph{ring} structure
53	    on \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\) and the graded
54	    \emph{module} structure on
55	    \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\).
56	\end{enumerate}
57	
58	\section{Tensor powers of a sheaf of modules}
59	\label{sec:sgr_tensor_powers}
60	
61	We work over a fixed scheme \(X\) (in the application \(X = X_s\), a fibre over a
62	field \(\kappa(s)\)). Its category of sheaves of modules is
63	\(X.\Modules = \ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\), with global-sections notation
64	\(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{M}) = \mathcal{M}(X)\) for the value of the underlying
65	presheaf on the top open. The structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_X\) is a sheaf of
66	commutative rings, so its underlying presheaf factors through the category of
67	commutative rings; this is the hypothesis under which Mathlib's presheaf-level
68	monoidal structure applies.
69	
70	\begin{lemma}[Monoidal structure on presheaves of modules]
71	  \label{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}
72	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategory}
73	  \mathlibok
74	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
75	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Presheaf.Monoidal}).}
76	  Let \(R : \mathcal{C}^{\mathrm{op}} \to \mathrm{CommRingCat}\) be a presheaf of
77	  commutative rings. Then the category \(\PshMod(R)\) of presheaves of modules
78	  over \(R\) carries a symmetric monoidal structure whose tensor product is
79	  computed objectwise: for presheaves of modules \(\mathcal{M}_1, \mathcal{M}_2\)
80	  and an object \(U\),
81	  \[
82	    (\mathcal{M}_1 \otimes \mathcal{M}_2)(U)
83	      \;=\; \mathcal{M}_1(U) \otimes_{R(U)} \mathcal{M}_2(U),
84	  \]
85	  with restriction maps the tensor products of the restriction maps. The unit is
86	  the presheaf \(R\) viewed as a module over itself, and the associator,
87	  unitors, and braiding are induced objectwise from those of
88	  \(\mathrm{ModuleCat}\). In particular evaluation at a fixed object \(U\) is a
89	  \emph{strict} monoidal functor
90	  \(\PshMod(R) \to \mathrm{ModuleCat}(R(U))\).
91	\end{lemma}
92	
93	\begin{lemma}[Sheafification of presheaves of modules]
94	  \label{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
95	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.sheafification}
96	  \mathlibok
97	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
98	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Presheaf.Sheafification}).}
99	  Let \(R\) be a sheaf of (commutative) rings on a site, with underlying
100	  presheaf \(R_0\). There is a sheafification functor
101	  \[
102	    (-)^{\#} \;:\; \PshMod(R_0) \longrightarrow \ShMod(R),
103	  \]
104	  left adjoint to the forgetful functor, together with the unit
105	  \(\eta_{\mathcal{M}_0} : \mathcal{M}_0 \to (\mathcal{M}_0)^{\#}\) of the
106	  adjunction. On underlying sheaves of abelian groups it agrees with the
107	  ordinary sheafification, and it sends a presheaf of \(R_0\)-modules to the
108	  associated sheaf of \(R\)-modules.
109	\end{lemma}
110	
111	\begin{lemma}[The structure sheaf as the unit module]
112	  \label{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}
113	  \lean{SheafOfModules.unit}
114	  \mathlibok
115	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
116	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Sheaf}).}
117	  For a sheaf of rings \(R\) (in the application \(R = \mathcal{O}_X\)) the
118	  structure sheaf, viewed as a module over itself, is a distinguished object
119	  \(\mathbf{1} = \mathcal{O}_X \in \ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\). It is the value at

286	\section{Lax-monoidal global sections}
287	\label{sec:sgr_lax_sections}
288	
289	The graded multiplication is supplied by the lax-monoidal structure of the
290	global-sections functor \(\Gamma(X, -) : \ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X) \to
291	\mathrm{ModuleCat}(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X))\). It is only \emph{lax} because
292	global sections do not commute with sheafification: a global section of a
293	sheafified tensor product is not in general a finite sum of elementary tensors of
294	global sections.
295	
296	\begin{definition}[Section multiplication]
297	  \label{def:sectionMul}
298	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.sectionsMul}
299	  \uses{def:sheafTensorObj, lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
300	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
301	  Let \(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G}\) be sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. The
302	  \emph{section multiplication} is the \(\Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X)\)-bilinear map
303	  \[
304	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_{\Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X)}
305	      \Gamma(X, \mathcal{G})
306	    \;\longrightarrow\;
307	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})
308	  \]
309	  defined as follows. A pair of global sections \((\sigma, \tau)\) determines,
310	  by the objectwise formula of \cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib} at the
311	  top open, the elementary tensor \(\sigma \otimes \tau \in
312	  (\mathcal{F} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})(X)\), a global section of
313	  the tensor product presheaf. Composing with the global-sections component of
314	  the sheafification unit
315	  \(\eta : \mathcal{F} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G} \to
316	  (\mathcal{F} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})^{\#} =
317	  \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}\)
318	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib},
319	  \cref{def:sheafTensorObj}) yields a global section
320	  \(\sigma \cdot \tau \in
321	  \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})\). Bilinearity over
322	  \(\Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X)\) is inherited from bilinearity of the elementary
323	  tensor and \(\Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X)\)-linearity of \(\eta\).
324	\end{definition}

326	\begin{lemma}[Coherence of the section multiplication]
327	  \label{lem:sectionMul_coherent}
328	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.sectionsMul_assoc_unit}
329	  \uses{def:sectionMul, lem:moduleUnit_mathlib, lem:sheafTensorPow_add}
330	  The section multiplications (\cref{def:sectionMul}) are associative and unital
331	  in the following sense. For sheaves \(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G}, \mathcal{H}\)
332	  the two composites
333	  \[
334	    \Gamma(\mathcal{F}) \otimes \Gamma(\mathcal{G}) \otimes \Gamma(\mathcal{H})
335	    \longrightarrow
336	    \Gamma\bigl((\mathcal{F} \otimes \mathcal{G}) \otimes \mathcal{H}\bigr)
337	    \cong
338	    \Gamma\bigl(\mathcal{F} \otimes (\mathcal{G} \otimes \mathcal{H})\bigr)
339	  \]
340	  agree after transport along the associator of \cref{def:sheafTensorObj}; and
341	  the unit element \(1 \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\)
342	  (\cref{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}) acts as a two-sided identity, i.e.\ the section
343	  multiplication by \(1\) is, after the unitor isomorphism
344	  \(\mathcal{O}_X \otimes \mathcal{F} \cong \mathcal{F}\), the identity on
345	  \(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})\).
346	\end{lemma}

348	\begin{proof}
349	  \uses{def:sectionMul, lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
350	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
351	  Both statements reduce to the corresponding identities at the level of the
352	  tensor product presheaf, where by \cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}
353	  evaluation at the top open is strictly monoidal, so the associator and unitors
354	  act on elementary tensors by the usual formulas
355	  \((\sigma \otimes \tau) \otimes \upsilon \mapsto
356	  \sigma \otimes (\tau \otimes \upsilon)\) and
357	  \(1 \otimes \sigma \mapsto \sigma\). The sheafification unit \(\eta\) is a
358	  natural transformation, so it commutes with the associator and unitor
359	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}); applying \(\Gamma(X,-)\)
360	  to the naturality squares transports these presheaf-level identities to the
361	  section multiplications.
362	\end{proof}

364	\section{Graded-ring and graded-module assembly}
365	\label{sec:sgr_graded_assembly}
366	
367	The components and multiplications of the previous two sections are assembled
368	into the graded ring and graded module through Mathlib's

370	abelian groups indexed by \(\mathbb{N}\) together with multiplication maps

373	\begin{lemma}[External-direct-sum graded commutative semiring]
374	  \label{lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib}

379	  monoids equipped with a graded multiplication
380	  \(A_i \times A_j \to A_{i+j}\) and a unit \(1 \in A_0\) satisfying graded

    lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}
  Fix \(m'\) and induct on \(m\). For \(m = 0\),
  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes 0} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'} =
  \mathcal{O}_X \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'} \cong \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}\)
  is the left unitor of the tensor product
  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}, descended through
  sheafification). For the step, using the recursive definition
  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+1)} = \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes \mathcal{L}\)
  and the associator of the tensor product,
  \[
    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+1)} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
    = (\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes \mathcal{L}) \otimes
      \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
    \cong \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes
      (\mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'})
    \cong \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes
      (\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'} \otimes \mathcal{L}),
  \]
  and the inductive comparison together with one more application of the
  recursive definition identifies this with
  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m + m')} \otimes \mathcal{L} =
  \mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m + m' + 1)}\). The commutativity and associativity
  constraints follow from the corresponding coherence pentagon and hexagon of
  the symmetric monoidal structure on \(\PshMod\)
  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}), which are preserved by the
  sheafification functor.
\end{proof}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{def:sheafTensorPow, def:sheafTensorObj,
    lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
  We do \emph{not} appeal to a monoidal structure on the whole category
  \(\ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\) --- none is available --- but only to the comparison
  on tensor \emph{powers} of the single invertible sheaf \(\mathcal{L}\). On such
  locally free factors the construction is elementary, and right-exactness of the
  tensor product is never invoked.

  \textbf{A local-iso criterion.} A morphism of sheaves of
  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules is an isomorphism as soon as it is one locally, i.e.\
  after restriction to each member of an open cover of \(X\): two-sided inverses
  defined over the members of a cover and agreeing on overlaps glue to a global
  inverse. We invoke this criterion \emph{only} on tensor products of sheaves
  that are locally free of rank one. On a trivialising open \(U\) where
  \(\mathcal{L}|_U \cong \mathcal{O}_U\), every tensor power restricts to
  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes k}|_U \cong \mathcal{O}_U\) as well; the tensor product
  presheaf there takes the value \(\mathcal{O}_X(U) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}
  \mathcal{O}_X(U) = \mathcal{O}_X(U)\); and the sheafification unit \(\eta\)
  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}) restricts over \(U\) to the
  identity. Hence over each such \(U\) the structural comparison maps used below
  --- the unitor, associator, and braiding of \cref{def:sheafTensorObj},
  descended objectwise from \cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib} --- are
  visibly bijective, and the criterion promotes them to genuine isomorphisms in
  \(\ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\). The subtlety that obstructs a general monoidal
  descent --- that the sheafification comparison
  \(\eta_{\mathcal P} \otimes \mathrm{id}\) need not be locally injective when
  \(\mathcal P\) is arbitrary, the tensor product being only right exact --- does
  not bite here: that object is never among the ones we test. The only maps we
  test are comparisons between locally free factors, where the unit is locally an
  identity and the comparison is a genuine local isomorphism.

  \textbf{Construction of \(\mu_{m,m'}\).} Fix \(m'\) and induct on \(m\). For
  \(m = 0\),
  \[
    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes 0} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
      = \mathcal{O}_X \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
      \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
  \]
  is the left unitor of \cref{def:sheafTensorObj}, an isomorphism by the
  criterion. For the inductive step the recursive definition
  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+1)} = \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes \mathcal{L}\)
  (\cref{def:sheafTensorPow}) gives
  \[
    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+1)} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
    = (\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes \mathcal{L}) \otimes
      \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
    \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes
      (\mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'})
    \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes
      (\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'} \otimes \mathcal{L}),
  \]
  using first the associator and then the braiding of \cref{def:sheafTensorObj},
  each an isomorphism by the criterion. A second associator and the inductive
  comparison \(\mu_{m,m'}\) whiskered by \(\mathcal{L}\) then identify the
  right-hand side with
  \[
    (\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}) \otimes
      \mathcal{L}
    \xrightarrow{\ \mu_{m,m'} \otimes \mathrm{id}\ }
    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m + m')} \otimes \mathcal{L}
    = \mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m + m' + 1)},
  \]
  the final equality being once more the recursive definition. The composite is
  \(\mu_{m+1,m'}\), an isomorphism as a composite of isomorphisms. Naturality in
  \(\mathcal{L}\) holds because each constituent comparison is natural.

  \textbf{Commutativity and associativity constraints.} Every map appearing in
  the construction is a structural comparison isomorphism between locally free
  factors, so the required coherences are equalities of morphisms that may be
  checked locally on a common trivialising cover. Over such an open every sheaf
  in sight becomes \(\mathcal{O}_U\) and every comparison map becomes the
  identity, whence both sides of the commutativity square (\(\mu_{m,m'}\) versus
  \(\mu_{m',m}\) postcomposed with the braiding) and of the associativity
  diagram (the two bracketings of
  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'} \otimes
  \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m''} \to \mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+m'+m'')}\)) reduce to the
  same local identity. Gluing the local equalities yields the global cocycle
  relations: the family \(\{\mu_{m,m'}\}\) is coherent in the
  pentagon/hexagon sense.

  \textbf{Remark on the principled route.} One could instead obtain \(\mu\) from
  a genuine monoidal structure on \(\ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\) descended from the
  presheaf-level monoidal structure (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib})
  along sheafification, via the localized-monoidal / reflective-localization
  template for sheaf categories. That principled construction is deferred: it is
  gated on closedness of the monoidal category of presheaves of modules (a
  monoidal-closed structure on \(\PshMod\)), which is not available at the pinned
  commit. The bespoke line-bundle construction above sidesteps that gap and is
  all the section graded ring requires.
\end{proof}
